data.title: LyondellBasell: All Eyes Are On Dividend Safety In 2025
data.description:
LyondellBasell Industries (NYSE:LYB) stock has experienced yet another challenging year in 2024 as the cyclical downturn in the petrochemical industry continues with full force.

Over the past 12-months alone, LYB fell by more than 20% and is now trading at levels last seen at the equity market bottom during the early days of the pandemic.

Consequently, LYB's dividend yield is now approaching 7%, which is the highest level on record if we disregard the temporary drop of the share price in early 2020.

What all that means is that LYB is now looking increasingly attractive for two distinct groups of investors - dividend investors seeking for high yields and contrarians looking to bet on the cyclical swing within the sector.

While I will focus on the former group here, the recent remarks by LYB management highlight the extent to which the macroeconomic headwinds have intensified in recent years.

There is no getting around it, 2024 was another challenging year for petrochemicals. (...) Across our key businesses, fourth quarter industry margins are about 60% of historical averages, underscoring the depth of the current downturn.

Source: LYB Q4 2024 Earnings Transcript

Pricing of key products has fallen sharply since 2021 and is now just slightly above the pre-pandemic levels of 2019.

At the same time, however, costs are materially higher following the recent surge in inflation and the level at which the oil prices have settled.

Based on all that, LYB margins are now significantly lower than their historical averages.

As one of the largest and highest margin players in the space, LYB is likely to be in a good position to reward investors who are willing to wait for demand for durable goods to recover and with that, prices to normalize.

However, the market is also worried that there are some important structural shifts in the industry relative to prior cycles. Namely, the slower growth globally and particularly in China, the higher energy costs, and regulatory changes in Europe. All that makes the notion that LYB margins would return to their prior cyclical highs a highly uncertain assumption for investors.

That is why, my investment thesis is now primarily focused on around LYB's dividend, and I am less willing to speculate on any potential cyclical upswing in favour of current investors. With a dividend yield of nearly 7% the only issue is just how safe the dividend payments are and what's the probability of a dividend cut in the coming years.

Focusing On Dividend Safety

One of the main starting points when evaluating LYB's dividend safety is what the company's management has been signalling over the past year through their capital allocation decisions.

In May of last year, LYB's management has made the decision to increase the dividend by an impressive 7%, which was the 14th consecutive annual increase. The reason why I use the word "impressive" is not so much because of the actual rate of increase, but rather due to the extremely high payout ratio on a GAAP basis (more on that later).

On top of all that, LYB's management is also increasing the cash allocated to capital expenditure, which is yet another strong signal of their conviction in the business turnaround.

For fiscal year 2024, Capex stood $1.5bn, which was around 112% of the annual depreciation expense. The outlook for FY 2025 is now for total Capex to reach $1.9bn, which is a meaningful increase from the prior period and well-above the sustaining levels.

When we look at GAAP figures for the past year, all that might sound as nonsense given the fact that LYB's net income has fallen below the annual dividend payments.

One-off Items

Once we exclude the one-off expenses for 2024 (identified items) then LYB's net income of $2.1bn is sufficient to cover the recently increased dividend payments, but the question of long-term sustainability remains.

The net income adjustments were related to lower of cost or market (LCM), gain on sale of business, asset write-downs and refinery exit costs with most of them occurring for the second year in a row, which could be a cause of concern when relying on adjusted figures.

By far the largest item, however, was related to asset write-downs in LYB's European assets during 2024 which is related to the company's strategic review within the region. These are non-cash items and as such bear no weight on the dividend safety, but it should be mentioned that the review process is still ongoing, which is likely to result in more write-downs or restructuring expenses in 2025.

The fourth quarter included identified items of $852 million net of tax. The items included non-cash write-downs related to our O&P Europe, Asia and International and Advanced Polymer Solutions segment of $769 million and $42 million, respectively, and costs incurred from plans to exit the refining business.

Source: LYB Q4 2024 Earnings Transcript

Planned Maintenance

In comparison to 2024, international operations should benefit from no planned maintenance in 2025, with the first quarter likely to be impacted by certain supply constrains in Germany.

In contrast to 2024, our O&P EAI segment has no major turnaround scheduled for the coming year. Nevertheless, feedstock supply for one of our German crackers is currently constrained due to unplanned downtime at a nearby refinery. With those constraints and ongoing soft market demand, we expect to operate our European assets at a rate of 75% during the first quarter.

Source: LYB Q4 2024 Earnings Transcript

But even after factoring these feedstock supply issues, European assets are expected to be operated at a rate of 75%, in comparison to 65% during the last reported quarter of 2023.

During the quarter, European markets remained weak with softer seasonal demand and lower consumer confidence. Polymer prices were modestly higher with an improved sales mix and stable naphtha feedstock costs. Due to the low demand, we operated our assets at rates of approximately 65% during the quarter.

Source: LYB Q4 2023 Earnings Transcript

The first quarter EBITDA would also be impacted by planned maintenance in the Olefins & Polyolefins Americas segment, with downtime being heavily weighted towards the first half of the year.

Refining

Yet another reason why Q1 of 2025 could be a rather unusual period in comparison to the rest of FY 2025 is the expected exit from the refining business. This is likely to be associated with certain one-off expenses, but the business will no longer be a headwind for profitability as it was in 2024.

Investor Takeaway

Following yet another disappointing year for LyondellBasell shareholders, the focus is shifting toward the highly attractive dividend yield of nearly 7%. After adjusting for non-cash items and taking into account expected operational developments in 2025, it appears that the dividend is safe for the time being. Margins are likely to gradually improve in 2025 and LYB's management confidence in terms of capital allocation further supports that.

data.title: The Kraft Heinz Surprise: Outshining Defensive Packaged Foods Over Next 5 Years
data.description:
One stock that has lagged the U.S. equity market rather badly over the last 10 years is The Kraft Heinz Company (NASDAQ:KHC). Amazingly, the quote has declined, despite the S&P 500 roughly tripling in price. Kraft Heinz was formed in July 2015 through a major merger, using the existing Heinz security as the combination entity. Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) has and continues to own a rather large stake, 325.6 million shares worth $10 billion currently. On the risk side of the equation, the business has been straddled with too much debt, preventing the stock price from rising materially. This is the quick-take summary to contemplate when buying/owning shares.

The good news is Kraft Heinz has slowly been tackling its debt problem, although the process has played out over many years. Today, the average retail investor and Wall Street analyst has kind of left the company for dead, as an investment with little "future" upside. However, I strongly disagree.

In fact, the latest share price selloff has opened a terrific 5.5% dividend yield, with very low valuations on the business being ignored by all. Believe it or not, Kraft Heinz has actually "outperformed" most all of the big food stocks over the past five years, and the next five look even more promising. Compared to peers/competitors General Mills (GIS), J. M. Smucker (SJM), Mondelez (MDLZ), PepsiCo (PEP), Hershey (HSY), Coca-Cola (KO), Campbell's Company (CPB), McCormick (MKC), Tyson (TSN), and Danone S.A. (OTCQX:DANOY), Kraft Heinz has absolutely held its own for shareholder value on a total return basis.

Measured from the $28 intraday quote reached last week, I am modeling a double in the stock price over the next five years to $56 is entirely possible, which would work out to a compounded gain of +20% annually when you include the dividend. In a technology-bust scenario, where the S&P 500 and NASDAQ 100 indexes proceed to flatline over the next five years (similar to the Dotcom bubble bursting after the year 2000), Kraft Heinz may actually morph into a must-own defensive stock for the patient, independent-thinking investor.

Let's review some of the logic to buy Kraft Heinz under $30. If you receive 5.5% in dividend payouts each year (a higher rate than available from cash savings or Treasury bond rates), while you pocket even minor price appreciation, "future" defensive-minded Kraft Heinz performance could prove a top choice with general Wall Street indexes trading at record overvaluations in February 2025.

Debt Ratios Improving

On a 10-year chart back to pre-merger Heinz shares, we can review how net financial debt (debt minus cash holdings) rose substantially on the merger in July 2015. Peaking near $31 billion in 2018, net debt is now down to $18.5 billion (at the end of December 2024). Even better news is operating cash flow is running at a rate of 5x total interest expense, up considerably from 3x in 2021 and less than 1x in early 2018. So, today's debt backdrop is the least risky since the merger commenced.

Debt to equity (40%) and total assets vs. liabilities (44%) are also near or at the lowest readings post-merger.

Cash flow to debt calculations have steadily improved over time. In this case, higher numbers showcase less debt risk overall. Rising from 0.1x in 2020 to 0.2x currently, Kraft Heinz has moved from the worst setup in the large-cap packaged food universe to a relatively average group operating condition over the last five years.

Further, cash flow to sales of 16.2% is a terrific margin. Kraft Heinz remains one of the leading food giants for margins on sales.

Favorable Valuation Setup

If new to Kraft Heinz as an investor, you would guess the valuation would be somewhere in the middle of the peer group, with financials improving over the last 10 years. You would have guessed incorrectly. Instead, Wall Street has moved on from KHC, while largely forgetting the bullish reasons to own it.

For example, the easily sustainable forward 2025 P/E is just 10.4x on cash earnings (adjusted from GAAP accounting). The packaged food leaders have a median average around 15x today.

When we include debt and cash holdings, an enterprise valuation can be compared/contrasted with the same zeroed-out debt setup as peers. The forward EV to EBITDA number of 8.7x is quite a distance from the 11x group median average.

EV to forward estimated sales is not a huge discount to the industry, but the 2.1x ratio is below average.

When reviewing a long list of fundamental data, Seeking Alpha's computer-ranking system gives the company an overall "A-" Quant Valuation Grade. Compared to its 5-year trading history and current industry valuations, Kraft Heinz qualifies as a much stronger choice for value investors than the credit it receives.

Cash Returns

Perhaps the most bullish Kraft Heinz datapoints to consider are the raw cash returns available for investors. The 8.57% trailing "free" cash flow return is the best in the large-cap packaged food sector. If you are hunting for a company with high rates of cash flow on your investment dollar to pay dividends, fix its balance sheet, and eventually grow the business again, KHC scores quite well.

The real-world cash distribution yield of 5.53% on a trailing basis is also the best upfront dividend rate in the major food peer group. The median average is closer to 3.5%.

But that's not all for the income investing crowd to get excited about. The current dividend yield is also 4.77x the equivalent rate from the S&P 500 index. Against 1.2% in average U.S. equity market yield for the blue-chip universe, Kraft Heinz is providing its best relative yield since the 2015 merger.

Final Thoughts

Wall Street analysts are very bummed about Kraft Heinz's future, projecting the recent past for results again in 2025, 2026, 2027. KHC has become the Rodney Dangerfield of the stock market, a company getting no respect.

My forecast is for cash EPS to rise better than 5% annually over the next five years from a little under $3 in 2025, partly riding higher-than-expected inflation rates.

Simplified, around $4 billion in cash flow yearly is the 2024-25 story. $1 billion is spent on necessary capital expenditure, and $2 billion is sent out as dividend checks, leaving $1 billion for discretionary management reinvestment. I would focus on repaying debt as my first priority. In five years, interest rates will be higher than today, as my view. Why refinance debt maturing at rising expense?

Additionally, if $5 billion were cut off the $18.5 billion net-debt reading today, Wall Street would undoubtedly pull the valuation of the business from one of the lowest-multiple operations up to the middle of the peer pack. For example, $13.5 in net debt by 2030 with annual cash flow of $5 billion (works out to 2.7 years of theoretical debt repayment to zero) would look far more conservative than 2024's $18.5 billion in debt divided by $4 billion in cash flow (4.6 years).

If Kraft Heinz can hike product sales with inflation and reduce net interest expense, cash EPS of $3.75 per share seems more credible for an estimate by 2030. Given a normalized P/E of 15x for the major food group, I am modeling $56 for a share quote in 60 months. And, this number with small dividend raises will likely deliver a still decent 3% to 3.5% dividend yield at that time, for new equity buyers.

It's honestly been a game of attrition for Kraft Heinz, working to pay down debt levels since late 2015. The company has sold off divisions, restructured operations, laid off workers, and spent as little as possible on CAPEX. My opinion is the next five years will prove much easier on the business, in terms of creating sales and EPS growth, with debt settings closer to industry averages in 2025.

What are the downside risks? I think my main worries are macroeconomic in nature. For instance, a stock market crash or prolonged bear market would affect the share quote to a degree. Even with a trading history of robust outperformance during sharp Wall Street drawdowns (for both Kraft Foods and Heinz before the merger), I would not expect KHC to experience significant gains in price under such a scenario. Plus, a deep recession (with Trump shaking the economy to its foundation, while U.S. debts are spiraling out of control) could mean sales and earnings struggle to maintain their current level.

However, with Berkshire Hathaway helping to run the business (with its 26.9% ownership stake), I am not overly concerned about management execution. Kraft Heinz has a very competitive position in the packaged food market, owning some of the top brands in the world. If you can exclude the debt load from your thinking, who doesn't want to own a high free cash flow and dividend yield machine, with great operating margins and consistent consumer staple demand for its products?

I rate Kraft Heinz shares a Buy under $30 for a 12-month outlook, and I am looking to enter a decent-sized position over the next few weeks.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Recent Rise Of The U.S. 10-Year Treasury Yield Is Still A Risk Factor For Stocks
data.description:
By James Picerno

The US 10-year Treasury yield is arguably the single-most important interest rate in the world, given its influence on a wide range of economic and financial activity. It’s certainly a key near-term risk factor for stocks in terms of offering a risk-free alternative.

If the 10-year yield is relatively elevated, it’s a compelling “risk-free” substitute to equities. In the long run, stocks are expected to be far more competitive, of course. But over shorter periods, which are buffeted by the give and take of greed and fear, a sharp rise or fall in the 10-year yield can influence the risk appetite for stocks.

The reasoning: the opportunity to lock in a competitive payout rate from the government, if it’s sufficiently high, cuts into the near-term appeal of the equity market’s higher but less-certain long-term expected return.

The challenge is deciding when the 10-year yield is high enough to pose a substantial near-term risk for stocks. That’s forever a speculative undertaking, and one that comes in many varieties, along with the usual caveats.

One possibility that’s worth regular monitoring is the 10-year yield’s relative level vs. its recent history. In particular, the top chart below tracks the 10-year rate in terms of its current percentage of its maximum level over the trailing 200-trading-day window.

By that measure, this 10-year yield indicator’s 95% reading (as of 3:00 pm eastern on Feb. 18) continues to signal elevated risk for stocks. Generally, a reading above 95% can be viewed as a warning. Like any risk metric, it’s hardly flawless as a predictor, although it does post an encouraging history of highlighting periods when the odds look relatively high(er) for a weak run in stocks in the near term.

The current 95% print is on the borderline that arguably separates “high” from “low/moderate” risk. The question is where the 10-year yield goes from here? As usual, there are many factors that could raise or lower the benchmark rate.

Factors on the short list to monitor include incoming data that colors the outlook for sticky inflation risk, which has been a recent driver behind the 10-year rate’s recent rise. As expectations for inflation increase, the crowd demands a higher yield premium as compensation for that risk.

Another variable to watch is the potential for an upswing in trade war risk as the White House weighs its options for imposing higher import tariffs per President Trump’s preferences for managing the nation’s global trading relationships.

To the extent that trade war risk rises, the appetite for a safe-haven asset could increase and thereby drive the 10-year rate lower as demand for bonds increases fixed-income prices (bond prices and yields move inversely).

Meantime, the 10-year yield appears to be straddling the fence, waiting for the next shoe to drop. Perhaps not surprisingly, the stock market is relatively calm at the moment, trading at/near its recent highs.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: NextEra Energy's Future: Balancing Renewable Expansion And Policy Risks
data.description:
Since his second time at the steering wheel of America, Donald Trump has signed various executive orders and implemented policies. The updated central energy policies that Trump is introducing will primarily work to grow fossil fuel extraction at home, while on the other side, they plan to cut down on regulation and diminish renewable energy funding. As it stands now, the Trump administration seeks to advance domestic oil and natural gas drilling operations, restore pipeline initiatives, and relax environmental restrictions to achieve energy sovereignty.

The new Government policies indicate that the administration will place less importance on government-backed incentives for solar and wind energy development, which is likely to hurt renewable energy market growth.

How will these policies benefit NextEra Energy (NYSE:NEE)? The regulatory environment for NextEra Energy brings both positive and negative aspects. Trump's pro-natural gas policy will likely help NextEra Energy expand its gas-fired power generation division, yet his decreased support for solar and wind could challenge their historical financial incentives and tax credit programs.

By focusing on natural gas development through its solar and battery storage units, NextEra can retain a competitive position. The deregulation process could possibly expedite project approvals through streamlined permitting, which would allow NextEra to expedite project timing and decrease costs.

The overall electricity demand has increased by just 9% during the previous few years, yet experts predict a substantial 55% growth in the coming twenty years. Moreover, Power and data consumption rates show historically high numbers, while manufacturing repatriation combined with city and car electrification act as strong drivers for this growth pattern. I think NextEra Energy stands ahead of most other companies when it comes to benefiting from this enormous power sector growth opportunity. NEE leads the surge of growing demand because it plays an active role in directing this rise in demand.

Also, Amazon (AMZN) plans to spend $100 billion on CapEx in 2025 after recently announcing a $10 billion data center investment in Mississippi again. With the increase in data center demand and the potential investment aimed to drive up demand for reliable, scalable, clean power, utilities, and energy providers, they need to offset that and invest in grid upgrades and complimentary renewable infrastructure. This means more opportunities for renewables for NextEra Energy to sign long‐term power purchase agreements, as well as finance new renewable energy and grid upgrades. At its core, as Amazon and other tech giants depend on ever-increasing amounts of clean, reliable power to support AI and cloud operations, utilities like NextEra should reap an amplified investment cycle in renewable energy and further improvements to energy infrastructure that serves that need.

Having said that, I will remain bullish on NextEra Energy due to its strong near-term outlook as well as a strong history of meeting or beating long-term guidance, which leaves me very confident in its earnings potential in the future. But it also lays out a clear capital allocation discipline line, a path that still grows in a challenging world of rising rates and Geopolitical uncertainty.

NextEra Stock Performance

Upon taking a close look at NextEra's stock performance, I must say it has been underwhelming compared to the whole energy sector, as the stock rallied only by 21% over the past 52 weeks, lagging behind the broader S&P 500 Utilities Index, which gained 31% in the same time frame. Currently, NEE trades near $68, down from its November 2024 high of $78.6 but up from a recent low of $66.9 on January 13. On the other hand, its biggest peer, Constellation Energy (CEG), has shown amazing performance, skyrocketing with triple-digit growth of around 146%. So here the question arises: why was NextEra lagging behind in the stock race?

Put simply, we can say that Several factors lead to the underperformance. NextEra Energy is very highly invested in renewable energy, which has regulatory and market risks, particularly in a volatile oil and gas market, where prices can change quickly, either up or down, based on geopolitical issues. For instance, Iran and USA conflict was seen in the past as a cause for major price hikes in oil prices. As a result, the whole energy stock volatility was also feathered. There is no doubt that NextEra is a leading player in clean energy, but the company also faces a bunch of challenges in the clean energy field, including large wind and solar projects hit with changing commodity prices, supply chain snarls, and permitting delays that may affect margins.

On the other hand, Constellation Energy benefits from a diversified business model that incorporates both conventional and clean energy. This business structure works by including a mix of traditional and clean energy, and it acts as a buffer against the varying price trends in the market for renewable energy that other energy companies suffer. It is also true that CEG grew fast because of the increase in the prices of natural gas, and energy security became much more urgent.

Meanwhile, we have also seen that NextEra has been snorting itself some cash with grid infrastructure long-term term projects, which has diminished its profit and, consequently, stock growth over the short run. I do feel that these new long-term projects, which the company is currently working on, will put the company on track to develop its product offerings to continue to grow the solar wind and storage solution services it offers long into the future. With NextEra's growing renewable energy backlog, which is increasing 30% per year, the company is well positioned to meet the market's energy demands. To expand the portfolio further, the company intends to rely on both major capital investment and creative partnerships, such as with GE Vernova in gas-fired generation.

For now, Investors could be holding off, waiting to see how well those big projects pan out.

Financial Overview

As we move into fundamentals, we can see that the company has paced up its growth very well. Over the past few years, it has consistently generated healthy revenue; in 2021, it earned $17 billion. In 2022, it hit $20 billion and reached $28.1 billion, marking more than a 40% year-over-year increase.

I believe one of the major reasons for this amazing growth was the company generated massive sales from its initial investments in wind and solar projects through both internal and external acquisitions. Florida Power & Light (FPL), a subsidiary of NEER, is now better able to spread the cost of higher reliability in Florida due to increased customer numbers, and NEER added 2,765 MW of new wind power, solar power, and battery storage into its backlog.

Furthermore, NEER added more than 3.2 GW of renewable projects during the fourth quarter of 2024, with solar accounting for 45%, storage representing 28%, wind repowering making up 14%, and new wind reaching 12%. This combined added an overall backlog of more than 21.5 GW. Additionally, the energy company is hoping to get more support from 860 MW data center power contract with Google (GOOG).

Despite all those appealing projects and support, NEE saw a major blow in full-year revenue of 2024. The sales decline from $28.1 billion to $24.7 billion. Investors might have worried about this dip, but the truth is this happened due to milder-than-predicted winter months and the loss of consumers on FPL's regulated subsidiary due to lower retail electricity sales. Reduced heating and cooling needs led to fewer sales at Florida Power & Light, and the same trend occurred at this utility, which resulted in overall lower revenue for the company. Another factor that we can say contributed to this decline is linked to the adjustments related to a unit of the sponsored company and associated company asset deals and business group changes in business groups, e.g., given formal consolidations of NextEra energy partners.

On the balance sheet, Aggressive scaling forced the company to breathe in more, and therefore, it increased its debt from around $20 billion in 2013 to nearly $100 billion by late 2024. This debt was enough for the company to invest in renewable energy, grid modernization, and battery storage, with only $1 billion in cash reserves on its balance sheet. This model is only possible to fund through good management of debt and accurate operating cash flow. Since early 2022, its operating cash flow has been over $10 billion, and it has been spending heavily on its large wind power, solar energy, and grid enhancement projects. However, the company's free cash flow remains in the negative figures due to its lot of new investments in renewable assets, including solar farms and battery storage facilities.

Now, coming into its latest quarterly earnings. On Jan 24, the leader in renewable energy reported its Q4 earnings print. Adjusted EPS was landed at $0.54, which came in line with analyst estimates and also marked a 2% increase year over year. The most worrying part is that the Q4 operating revenues saw a huge decline of 21% to $5.38 billion and also missed the estimate by more than $2.5 billion. This revenue dip was mainly due to underperformance in core segments, including Florida Power & Light's revenue, which fell to $3.86 billion. Moreover, I have already discussed while mentioning the full-year sales decline that adjustments from asset sales and restructuring of business segments contributed to the decline. Overall, these operational challenges hit revenue, and income declined badly.

However, these are not all negatives for this quarter. NextEra Energy added nearly 3.3 GW of contracted renewables and storage projects, roughly 1,200 MW of wind, 1,000 MW of solar, 1,000 MW of battery storage, and 100 MW from wind repowering, pushing its backlog beyond 25 GW and including plans to explore recommissioning the Duane Arnold Energy Center. These long-term power purchase agreements also reduce the market volatility risk because of this aggressive expansion into wind, solar, and battery storage projects. Meanwhile, big tech is increasing its investments in data centers, and NextEra is looking forward to devoting about $120 billion in the next four years to supporting the expansion of energy capacity to 121 gigawatts. The focus states that part of this capital will be used to recommission the Duane Arnold nuclear plant in Iowa, which could go online by the end of 2028, but also to deliver additional natural gas generation solutions.

NextEra Storm-Resilient Grid Performance

NextEra Energy Subsidy Florida Power & Light (FPL) continues to distinguish itself as a best-in-class utility. Due in large part to the long history of commitment to smart grid technology, the company executed exceptionally well during Hurricanes Debby, Helene, and Milton in 2024. During a stormy year in 2024, FPL avoided more than 2.7 million outages because it made investments over the last 20 years to create a storm-resilient grid. Additionally, FPL maintains customer bills at 40% below the national average through low-cost solar generation and battery storage that has saved over $16 billion in fuel costs from 2001 until today, and nonfuel O&M costs per customer are 70 percent less expensive to customers, meaning customers save over $3 billion per year.

In late 2024, FPL submitted a test year letter to the Florida Public Service Commission, initiating a rate proceeding set to establish new rates starting in January 2026. To the extent FPL is expected to sign a new base rate agreement covering 2026 to 2029, the company is due for a 10% increase in 2024 regulatory capital employed. Between 2022 and 2025, FPL invested $36 billion to develop a robust and resilient power grid for Florida. Recovering these long-term investments will be aided by the new base rate adjustments to an average of 2.5% year after year, which will keep residential customer bills flat overall on average per year so that by January 2026, an average FPL customer bill will remain 21% below real terms compared to 20 years ago. As a higher level of interest rates, FPL also has a midway ROE of 11.9%.

Valuation Metrics

When evaluating NextEra Energy based on forward earnings, the company's outlook appears solid. Analysts forecast steady growth in earnings per share, supported by its robust cash flows and strong operational performance. This forward-looking stability suggests that NEE is likely to generate consistent, albeit conservative, earnings in the future.

Turning to valuation, NextEra is currently trading at 18.5 times forward earnings, which is marginally above the sector median of 16.5. However, it trades at significantly lower than most of its biggest competitors. For instance, Constellation Energy Corporation trades at 37.6x and Vistra Corp at 29.7 forward earnings. This lower P/E ratio of NextEra indicates that the market expects NEE to deliver earnings with less volatility and risk, which is appealing in the utility sector known for stable cash flows and strong balance sheets.

Another key metric, the forward Price-to-Sales ratio for NEE, stands at 4.9, while both Constellation and Vistra have ratios of 4.2 and 3.6, respectively. The elevated premium indicates investors allocate more funds for each revenue dollar generated by NEE since they trust its operational excellence and market position as well as its customer retention abilities.

Investors seem to view NextEra Energy as lower-risk, but the elevated revenue premium demands people to analyze both its revenue growth prospects and margin sustainability before investing.

Risk To The Thesis

As we all know, no investment is completely risk-free. So, I have pointed out a few risks that investors should consider when investing in this energy stock company. I think Heavy debt levels exist as a primary risk for NextEra. The company relies on massive $72.39 billion debt funding combined with more than $2 billion annual interest to finance investments in renewable energy grid modernization and batteries. The Fed's rate increase would create substantial increases in the cost of debt servicing, which would cut into the profitability of the company's renewable project.

Additionally, Trump's policies intend to produce more fossil fuels while lowering energy prices, but this political strategy contains risks because market conditions could shift suddenly whenever policy makes changes from the governing administration or if administration policies change. Meanwhile, The company successfully operates two simultaneous business divisions, where its regulated energy utility business operates alongside riskier renewable energy operations. While renewable energy operations promise future business expansion, they face challenges from price volatility, supply challenges, and regulatory adjustments. Nevertheless, as said, long-term trends favor clean energy.

Another major risk is that the International Energy Agency projects that fossil fuel demand will peak by 2030 and that renewables will overtake coal as the primary source of electricity by 2025. This means that the demand for companies like NextEra, which generates electricity from solar farms and natural gas, will be reduced, which could be challenging for these types of companies.

Takeaway On NextEra Stock

From my point of view, NextEra Energy presents an appealing future outlook because of its reliable operational results and exceptional clean energy business. Moreover, NextEra's stock valuation also presents an attractive entry point, as the stock is currently trading at nearly a 6-month low and has a low P/E ratio.

There is no doubt that there will be some challenges from its rapid expansion with receiving high leverage alongside regulatory uncertainties along with possible technological disruptions may harm its short-term results, but the company's success in the future depends on striking a balance between aggressive growth of renewable sources and disciplined monetary management because we have observed continuous guidance achievement by management.

Adding further positive momentum, On Jan 27, NextEra Energy announced a partnership with GE Vernova for load growth benefits alongside filing to revive the Duane Arnold nuclear plant with 600 MW capacity. This will also act as a positive catalyst for the stock in the near future.

data.title: Does The Gold Bull Run Have Further To Go?
data.description:
By Sachin Patel

At a Glance

  • Global instability, central bank purchases and AI demand could continue to impact gold prices
  • Silver prices could find strength amid rising industrial demand and a high gold-silver ratio

Gold has enjoyed a spectacular bull run in the past year, with prices jumping by nearly 30% since January 2024. Factors like geopolitical and economic uncertainty could continue to support gold's rally this year, though the potential for fewer rate cuts and a stronger U.S. dollar could put downward pressure on prices.

The Case for Gold

As of early February, the price of gold is around US$2,873/oz, surpassing October's record high of just under US$2,788/oz according to data from Gold Price. A combination of factors has come together to create an extremely supportive environment for gold, with global instability, including the conflicts in Ukraine and the Middle East, and increased purchasing by central banks, particularly in China, India and other emerging economies, all making the yellow metal shine for investors.

Meanwhile, gold could find further support from increased policy uncertainty in the U.S. Outside of the U.S., political instability in Korea and France could further enhance the metal's appeal as a safe haven asset.

Gold and U.S. Policy

Despite these supportive conditions, there are some potential clouds gathering on the horizon for gold. Gold dropped to a near two-month low following the U.S. election.

A number of factors contributed to the decline. In the short term, equities have appeared more attractive to investors as they anticipate an upcoming period of pro-business policies, such as reduced tax and regulation. At the same time, the U.S. dollar recently hit a two-year high.

Gold traditionally has a negative correlation with the U.S. dollar, as a strong dollar makes gold purchases more expensive for overseas buyers. However, it should be noted that this correlation has uncoupled recently, with both the U.S. dollar and gold increasing in value last year - the former on the strength of the U.S. economy and the latter on expectations of interest rate cuts and ongoing global uncertainty.

Although October's U.S. jobs report pointed to a weaker-than-expected labor market, much of the weakness was attributed to one-off factors such as hurricanes and major strikes, and November's data showed a strong rebound, reducing the urgency for interest rate cuts. Expectations that interest rate cuts may be slower than expected has, in turn, impacted bond yields, making gold seem relatively less attractive to investors.

On the other hand, expectations that the U.S. budget deficit could increase could provide some support for gold. A growing budget deficit, and by extension an increasing national debt, risks undermining confidence in the economy, which could in turn increase investors' appetite for safe haven assets like gold. Concern about the U.S. economy could also weaken the U.S. dollar, further increasing the appeal of gold.

Strong Global Demand

Outside of the U.S., purchasing trends are still expected to remain positive for gold going forward, providing further support. In its Q3 Gold Demands Trends report, the World Gold Council pointed to the strongest increase in Q3 gold demand since its series began, with total demand exceeding $100bn for the first time. The report, which pre-dated the U.S. election result, showed demand from investors more than doubled year-on-year, driven by the need for portfolio protection and diversification in the face of ongoing economic and geopolitical uncertainty, an outlook that is not likely to change. Demand was particularly strong among ETFs and western investors. Although central bank buying eased somewhat, it remained robust and in line with the same period of 2022.

At the same time, the artificial intelligence boom continued to drive demand for gold for use in technology. The only fall recorded was in demand for jewelry, which dropped 12% by volume due to the high price of gold, although high prices meant demand by value was actually up by 13%. Overall, total global gold demand increased 5% year-on-year to 1,313t in the third quarter.

Is Silver Also Having a Moment?

Silver, which had been enjoying a strong rally driven by a combination of rising industrial demand and supply constraints, also suffered a price dip in the aftermath of the U.S. election. The price of silver, which is both an industrial and a precious metal, has increased by 33% since the start of 2025. Although its path has been choppier than gold's, its October high was within touching distance of prices not seen for more than a decade.

Silver is benefiting from strong demand from the industry. In particular, growth in the electric vehicle sector, which uses silver in batteries and charging infrastructure. High demand for EVs is expected to be a significant driver for the silver price going forward. Meanwhile, supply-side constraints could also help to push prices higher, with the amount of silver mined in 2024 estimated to be marginally lower than the previous year, according to the Silver Institute.

The metal also faces some headwinds. Like gold, silver typically has an inverse correlation with the U.S. dollar. As a result, the recent strength of the dollar could continue to put downward pressure on price gains, at least in the short term. Over the longer term, its outlook will also be impacted by the pace of interest rate cuts.

Meanwhile, the gold-silver ratio remains at relatively high levels by historical standards. The ratio, which measures the price of an ounce of gold divided by the price of an ounce of silver, stands at almost 89 as of mid-January. Historically, when the ratio has been above 80, silver has gone on to enjoy a strong rally, which could mean silver still has plenty of runaway should gold continue to perform in 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: EPI: Tariffs, Ebbing Economic Growth, And AI Are Threats (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
India was a hot trade for much of the recovery period off the COVID-19 lows almost five years ago. The past seven months have been a different story, at least on a relative basis to the rest of the world, however. In a research note published earlier this week by Goldman Sachs, the YTD country performance table revealed that India was the standout performer in a negative light.

Across sectors, the emerging market stock index had poor returns. Tariff fears, threats from AI on India’s workforce, and somewhat sagging economic data have all weighed on its equity index. As such, we’ve seen the WisdomTree India Earnings Fund (NYSEARCA:EPI) underperform the iShares All-Country ex-US ETF (ACWX) by more than 20 percentage points since August 2024.

I had a buy rating on EPI more than a year ago, but shares have returned –3%, sharply underperforming the S&P 500. Today, I’m downgrading the product to a hold after a stinker of a year. I’d like to see economic data and price action improve before turning bullish again. I will provide prospective investors with a key technical price point that I assert would be a favorable risk/reward entry spot.

India: Weak YTD Sector Returns

Global Tariff Rates

GS: India: Manufacturing PMI declined to an 11-month low in November; output price index at an 11-year high

According to the issuer, EPI seeks to track the price and yield performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree India Earnings Index. Investors use EPI to gain exposure to broad Indian equities across the market cap spectrum with a valuation-centric approach.

EPI is a moderate-sized ETF with $3.0 billion in assets under management as of February 14, 2025. That’s actually up from just $2.3 billion at the time of my February 2024 analysis. It carries a high 0.87% annual expense ratio, while the forward dividend yield is low at 0.29%, more than a full percentage point below that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has turned south in a big way given India’s lackluster YTD performance; it has an F rating by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. EPI also ranks poorly in terms of risk, given high annualized volatility lately and a concentrated portfolio. Finally, liquidity is the lone bright spot as the product averages more than 1 million shares traded daily while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at just two basis points, per WisdomTree.

For a portfolio update, the 4-star-rated ETF by Morningstar plots along the top row of the style box, indicating its high exposure to India’s large-cap market. Just 10% of the fund is considered mid-cap, and there is no small-cap access. So, from a size perspective, EPI should feature less cyclical risk compared to other ETFs that are spread out across the style box.

Despite the negative year-on-year performance, EPI’s price-to-earnings ratio remains near 15x while the long-term EPS growth rate is high. If we see growth trends play out, then I would not be surprised to see EPI feature a deep-value construct later this year.

EPI: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

With a low valuation multiple but protracted negative alpha to the global stock market, EPI’s portfolio is heavy into Financials, which has been a winning area, and Energy, which has been particularly weak in recent months.

Information Technology is just 14% of the allocation, significantly below I.T.’s weight in the SPX, for instance. Utilities and Materials, along with Energy, are significant overweights versus the ACWI.

EPI: Holdings & Dividend Information

In terms of seasonality, February has been EPI’s worst month on the calendar. Better performance trends have been seen from March through May over the past 10 years, however.

EPI: Better Springtime Seasonality Ahead

The Technical Take

Amid soft absolute and relative returns lately and with a favorable valuation, EPI’s technical chart points to more downside ahead. Notice in the graph below that the fund is now approaching its October 2021 peak, just above the $39 mark. I also inspected Fibonacci retracement levels, finding that the $36 to $37 range should have material support. So, buying near $37, below the 2021 peak, could be a favorable risk/reward play. That would imply, however, another 10% of downside from today’s price.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart – it printed a series of lower highs throughout 2024 before EPI eventually peaked in price last September. Following a bearish death cross in which the short-term 50-day moving average crossed below the longer-term 200dma, the bears control the primary trend. What’s more, the RSI zone is now between 20 and 60, a bearish area. On the upside, resistance is seen in the $47-$48 range.

EPI: High $30s Support, Weak Momentum Trends, Bearish Death Cross

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on EPI. Once a thriving ETF, the trend has turned in favor of the bears. The valuation case is there, but soft economic data put near-term growth opportunities at risk. Finally, the technicals are weak heading into what is usually a decent seasonal period.

data.title: Japan's Export Jump May Be Calm Before Trumpian Storm
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang

Exports remain solid in January, but the outlook is clouded by mounting tariff threats

Japanese exports rose 7.2% year-on-year in January (vs 2.8% in December, 7.7% market consensus).

The US was the standout destination for Japan Inc., with overseas shipments to the globe’s biggest economy posting a strong 8.1% increase. This, however, appears due to front-loading before the full scope of US President Trump’s plans for tariffs. Exports to China fell 6.2%, probably due to the Lunar New Year holiday. Despite weak exports to China, shipments to Asia and ASEAN rose 6.3% and 15.6%, respectively. Regional commerce is showing healthy gains, and we believe that the rerouting of China trade flows may be one reason for the recent strength in interregional exports.

Transportation equipment, the largest export category, grew a solid 12%. Ships surged 106.9%, while motor vehicle exports increased 10.5%. Automakers in particular appear to be rushing to ship products ahead of US levies on cars. We suspect medical products jumped a robust 23.4% for similar reasons.

Meanwhile, imports rose a bigger-than-expected 16.7% (vs 1.7% in December, 9.3% market consensus). This surge led to a trade deficit of -2,758 trillion JPY. Imports of capital goods rose strongly. Overall machinery and electrical machinery orders, for example, increased 46.9% and 18.2%, respectively. This bodes well for rising capital investment in the current quarter.

Transportation equipment rose solidly in January

Core machinery orders fell for the first time in three months

Separately, core machinery orders unexpectedly fell by 1.2% month-on-month, seasonally adjusted (vs 3.4% in Nov, 0.5% market consensus), after solid increases in the previous two months. But when smoothing out monthly volatility, core machinery growth accelerated to 2.9% quarter-on-quarter, seasonally adjusted, in December from -1.3% in September. The January increase in imports of capital goods and core machinery orders may offer rays of hope following Monday's gross domestic product report, which saw a drop in imports. But amid great uncertainty about the Trumpian storm to come, Japan's 2025 outlook remains cloudy.

Core machinery orders point to a recovery in investment in the current quarter

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: AMD: Betting On The Next AI Chip To Boost Sales Momentum
data.description:
AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) (TSX:AMD:CA) stock has been facing bearish sentiment for the last few quarters. The recent earnings did not do much to change the sentiment towards the stock. All eyes were on the data center revenue, and the company missed the consensus estimate by a wide margin. AMD reported $3.86 billion in data center revenue, which is 69% YoY growth but significantly lower than the $4.14 billion consensus estimate. This segment includes the important AI chip sales, and the management did not give any numbers about the future growth trajectory of its AI chips. In the previous article, it was mentioned that AMD's earnings momentum can pick up as more flexible options are used by the hyperscalers.

One reason behind the lack of forward guidance for AI revenue could be the DeepSeek shock. Most of the big tech spenders have not changed their massive capex plans, but Wall Street is clearly showing a greater preference towards prudent spending. Many of the Mag 7 stocks have not done well after their earnings results and one of the reasons is the massive capex plans. AMD will need to carve a niche in this shifting AI story. Its next-generation AI chips are projected to be quite powerful and at the right price point could improve the trajectory of sales for these chips.

AMD is trading at 17.8 times the EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026. This is quite low when we look at the potential for growth in the next few quarters. The EPS growth momentum is also quite strong. The stock price is quite low and any positive news within its AI chip segment could turbo-boost the stock price, making it a good bet within the AI industry.

Earnings fail to impress

AMD’s stock has seen a bearish sentiment for the past few months, and the recent earnings did not change this trend. Despite beating the revenue estimate, the stock corrected after the earnings result was announced.

Figure: Recent earnings of AMD. Source: Seeking Alpha

The quarter-on-quarter revenue growth within the Data Center segment was only 9%. This is quite low compared to the earlier quarters. For YoY, AMD announced a massive 69% growth. This clearly shows that the Q4 2024 quarter was quite slow for the Data Center segment. The company was able to deliver higher operating income QoQ growth of 11% which shows that the gross margin in this segment is improving.

Figure: AMD’s data center segment results. Source: AMD filings

Figure: AMD’s results for the full year. Source: AMD filings

Besides the lower-than-expected results, Wall Street also did not like the lack of clear guidance from the management. AMD CEO did mention that the company was looking to increase the AI chip revenue from $5 billion in 2024 to “tens of billions of dollars annually within a few years”. However, this is quite different from the previous estimate given by the management, where they mentioned exact AI chip revenue and were able to beat their own guidance.

AI industry is in flux

The AI industry is in a massive flux. After the DeepSeek shock, most of analysts have started questioning the need for massive capex by big tech companies. This is reflected in the poor performance of many Mag 7 stocks after the recent earnings. Recently, researchers from Stanford and the University of Washington were able to build AI “reasoning” models with a modest $50 cloud compute credits. This clearly shows that a lot of efficiency is possible within the AI industry. Some of these models use a distillation process, but their results have been as good as other OpenAI’s o1 and DeepSeek’s R1 models.

Figure: Massive capex by big tech is now higher than the peak Big oil capex-to-revenue share. Source: The Information

A recent research report by The Information mentioned how the capex-to-revenue share of big tech companies is now higher than the peak capex-to-revenue share of Big Oil, which is a capex-heavy industry.

None of the big tech top executives have mentioned a possible reduction in capex. But it is likely that if Wall Street continues to punish these stocks in the next few months, we could see a slower capex trajectory from these big spenders.

Betting on the next AI iteration

An important information in this earnings call was the announcement of launching Instinct MI350 series chips by mid-year. The management mentioned that there was positive feedback from key customers, which has led to the company pulling the production ramp to the middle of 2025. AMD does not have the moat of NVIDIA’s (NVDA) CUDA platform, but it provides cheaper chips. If the big tech companies focus more on efficiency over the next few quarters, it could help AMD carve a good niche for its lower-cost AI chips.

Figure: Forward revenue estimates for AMD. Source: Seeking Alpha

We can see the potential of future AI chips in the forward revenue estimates of AMD. Data Center segment already makes up close to half of the total revenue base of AMD. If the next iteration of AI chips performs well, they could significantly boost the revenue and earnings trajectory of the company. The high revenue estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 is $45 billion, which is over 80% higher than the current revenue base. The margins of AI chip sales are very high and any stronger-than-expected sales should help in boosting the EPS trajectory of the company.

Future stock momentum

AMD stock is trading at a very modest price after the bearish trend of the last few months. The current stock price is close to 50% lower than the peak of $207 in early 2024. While the recent quarter did not match expectations in the data center segment, we could expect a better result as the next iteration of AI chips comes online. The stock is trading at only 17.8 times the consensus EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026. This is quite low for a company that could deliver strong AI chip sales in the next few quarters.

Figure: Forward EPS estimate of AMD. Source: Seeking Alpha

Figure: AMD's and Nvidia's forward EPS comparison

AMD's forward PE multiple is significantly below Nvidia. The expectations from AMD are quite low, which makes it easier for the company to beat the estimates in the near term. Any shift towards more value-oriented chips within the AI industry will be a major tailwind for AMD’s chips, which are priced significantly lower than the flagship chips of Nvidia.

Investor Takeaway

AMD did not meet the expectations of Wall Street in its Data Center segment, which includes the important AI chips sales. This was one of the reasons behind the bearish sentiment towards the stock after the earnings. The QoQ growth rate in the data center segment was only 9% compared to 69% YoY growth rate, which shows a bit of a slowdown in sales. However, AMD is planning to launch the next iteration of AI chips MI350 by mid-year, which should improve the trajectory within this segment.

AMD offers lower-priced chips compared with Nvidia, which could be a benefit if the industry looks for more value-oriented options. The forward expectations are quite modest and AMD is in a good position to beat the estimates, which should improve the sentiment and also improve the multiples of the stock.

data.title: Will Positive Stock-Bond Correlations Fade Due To Sticky Inflation?
data.description:
By James Picerno

The case for a “diversified” portfolio relies primarily on the complementary relationship between stocks and bonds. While equities are expected to deliver the lion’s share of performance most of the time, bonds provide a hedge for those times when stock prices are falling. In the parlance of statistics, stocks and bonds exhibit low or negative return correlation.

That, at least, is the general view. But like many casual assumptions about financial markets, reality is more nuanced, especially over shorter-term periods. The caveat applies to correlations for stocks and bonds.

The diversification benefits of holding stocks and bonds endures, but the degree varies through time. There are periods when this form of diversification fades, and we are in one of those periods currently.

Since mid-2022, the daily return correlation between stocks and bonds has been moderately positive, based on a pair of index funds (see chart below). That reflects a stark shift after roughly two decades of mostly negative correlations, based on rolling 200-day windows for daily returns.

The lesson is that correlations ebb and flow, which is to say that the degree of diversification you receive from holding stocks and bonds is constantly in flux. Negative correlations align with stocks and bonds moving independently of each other, which can be helpful when one asset class is suffering.

To be fair, even a moderately positive correlation (as currently prevails) still offers diversification benefits. The latest 0.14 correlation is positive, but only slightly, which is to say it’s still a long way from approaching 1.0, or perfect positive correlation – a state that would, if it persisted, offer no diversification benefits.

The larger point is that diversification’s efficacy as a risk-management tool via stocks and bonds is far from static. Reflecting on this evolving relationship is timely for a couple of reasons.

First, diversification generally is becoming harder to achieve at a time of globalized markets. Because money can easily move across borders and asset classes with the click of a computer mouse, the barriers have fallen that once kept markets relatively isolated (and correlations lower).

In turn, investors are more inclined and able to move in lockstep at times as news and information circles the globe in a flash. There are still differences between asset classes and markets, of course, but those differences have generally narrowed. Diversification, as a result, has become more challenging to achieve.

But while diversification may no longer be as robust compared with decades past, it’s still available as a foundational tool for managing risk and boosting risk-adjusted performance. One thing that hasn’t changed: correlation still goes through cycles, mainly due to the changing nature of macro factors.

A key set of drivers: economic growth and inflation. A 2023 study in The Journal of Portfolio Management reports that the stock–bond correlation “depends not on the level of inflation, but on the relative volatility of growth and inflation and the correlation between them.”

Meanwhile, “We have become accustomed to a negative correlation between stocks and bonds, but this was not the historical norm prior to the 2000s, with the average correlation positive in the 20th century,” write the authors of “A Changing Stock–Bond Correlation: Drivers and Implications” in the March 2023 issue of JPM.

Regardless of the explanation for changing correlation regimes, history is clear that the stock-bond correlation is dynamic. That empirical fact implies that the recent run of moderately positive correlations is probably a temporary phase. Why? Growth and inflation trends are in flux, which in turn influences correlation.

In the current climate, when the Federal Reserve appears to be increasingly focused on so-called sticky-inflation risk, a shift in the stock-bond correlation relationship may be approaching.

This may turn out to be a significant issue if US economic growth remains relatively robust. In that scenario, there’s a possibility that the central bank will raise interest rates, which implies that the stock-bond correlation will decline, perhaps returning to negative terrain once again.

If correct, this outlook implies that bonds could suffer relative to stocks. Assuming stocks continue rallying, or least exhibit relative strength vs. bonds, this is a recipe for a lesser return correlation. This is also the basis for adjusting asset allocation and favoring inflation-indexed bonds in some degree vs. nominal bonds.

The market appears to be picking up on this assumption. For example, the iShares TIPS Bond ETF (TIP) is outperforming its near-equivalent Treasuries counterpart (IEF) so far this year through Feb. 14: 1.9% vs. 1.3%.

Perhaps, then, it’s no surprise that stock-bond correlations have recently fallen from the peaks of the last couple of years. The current 0.14 correlation is roughly half the level of the peaks in 2023 and 2024. If inflation remains sticky, even lower correlations may be approaching.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: VTIP: Short-Term Treasury TIPs, Inflation-Protected Dividends, Peer Outperformance
data.description:
The Vanguard Short-Term Inflation-Protected Securities Index Fund ETF (NASDAQ:VTIP) is an index EFT investing in treasury inflation-protected securities, or TIPs. TIPs are indexed to inflation, and see higher coupon rates and returns when inflation is high and rising, with inflation running at slightly above-average levels right now. VTIP currently yields around 1.7% plus inflation, currently 3.0%, for a 4.7% yield to maturity or expected return. Amounts here seem competitive to peers, especially considering VTIP's risk profile - extremely low credit risk, low rate risk. VTIP's safe holdings, good expected returns, and effectiveness as an inflation hedge, make the fund a buy. Considering its characteristics,

VTIP - Yield and Prospective Return Analysis

TIPs are something of a niche asset class, so thought to start with a quick explanation of how these work. I have a longer explanation here.

TIPs are treasury inflation-protected securities. As with normal treasuries, TIPs are issued by the U.S. Treasury, backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. government, and pay a fixed coupon rate. Current rates are as follows:

Unlike normal treasuries, TIPs are protected against, or indexed to, inflation. Specifically, their (face) value is pegged to the CPI, and increases as prices do. Deflation can erase prior gains, but never result in net losses (because these securities are simply not structured for that). In general, the gains from inflation are classified as income, and are distributed to shareholders as dividends. So, higher inflation means higher dividends, and vice versa. Considering prevailing coupon rates, that means these securities yield around 1.7% - 2.4% plus inflation right now. For VTIP itself, figures are closer to 1.7% plus inflation.

Due to the vagaries of ETF distribution requirements, significant inflation, rate, and bond price volatility, individual dividend payments / yields might not perfectly align with the figures above, but the overall trend is clear. VTIP's dividend yield has moved alongside inflation rates these past few years, for instance.

Coupon rates for these securities are at historically above-average levels, due to prior Fed hikes. Coupon rates below do not include the inflation component.

This is a straightforward benefit for investors, albeit one shared by most other types of bonds: rates are high across the board, not just for TIPs.

Looking at inflation rates, CPI last averaged 3.0%, implying around 4.7% in total returns for VTIP considering prevailing TIPs rates. Remember, the fund leans short-term, so the 5y figure is the most relevant.

Obviously, changes to inflation would change VTIP's dividends and returns, so consider that 4.7% a highly tentative figure. The Federal Reserve aims for a 2.0% inflation rate, implying 3.7% in returns for the fund.

Notwithstanding the above, VTIP only yields 2.7% right now. Unclear why yields are lower than expected considering the figures above, but this is incredibly common for TIPs ETFs, as these have many moving parts.

VTIP - Benefits

Inflation Hedge

VTIP offers investors some protection against inflation, as the fund's dividends grow as inflation increases. VTIP's returns are impacted by other factors too, including Fed rates, market interest rates, and expectations thereof. Interest rates tend to increase when inflation rises, blunting the fund's inflation protection, but not eliminating it completely.

As an example, VTIP suffered losses of around 2.9% during 2022, a year of skyrocketing inflation. VTIP's inflation protection meant significantly reduced losses relative to most treasuries, but there were still some losses.

Medium-term returns are stronger, however.

VTIP provides investors with some protection against inflation, especially in comparison to broader bond funds, an important, straightforward benefit for investors.

Low Risk and Volatility

VTIP focuses on short-term treasury inflation-protected securities, with an average duration of only 2.4 years. It is low on an absolute level, and lower than that of most bonds and bond sub-asset classes.

TIP focuses on short-term treasury inflation-protected securities, issued by the U.S. Treasury, and backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. Government. Credit risk is effectively zero, barring an unprecedented U.S. default.

TIP focuses on short-term treasury inflation-protected securities, so the fund is somewhat protected against inflation, as explained above. Said protection adds something of safety to the fund, even though it might not be readily reflected in most metrics (volatility, drawdowns, etc.).

As should be clear from the above, VTIP is an incredibly low-risk, low-volatility investment, much less risky than the average bond ETF.

Attractive Expected Returns

As mentioned previously, current coupon and inflation rates imply around 4.7% in returns for VTIP moving forward. Actual returns might significantly differ from these figures for many reasons, chief among these a material change in inflation. Still, that 4.7% figure is a reasonable expectation, and slightly higher than treasury yields across the board.

As an aside, one can calculate breakeven inflation rates from the above. TIPs currently yield around 1.7% plus inflation, so TIPs should outperform most normal treasuries if inflation averages higher than 2.6% these next few years. Returns are dependent on other factors too, but this figure is a reasonable expectation and based on current market rates. Inflation currently stands at 3.0%:

As an aside, I did a similar calculation to the above in a prior article and arrived at a 2.8% break-even inflation figure. Inflation has generally been higher since:

With VTIP outperforming most treasuries, including those of comparable duration, since, as expected.

Circling back, VTIP's expected returns are currently higher than those of most treasuries, an important, albeit uncertain, benefit.

Good Performance Track Record

VTIP's performance track-record is quite good, with the fund outperforming most bonds and bond sub-asset classes since inception, and for most time periods. Outperformance was due to the fund's inflation protection, beneficial when inflation spiked, and its below-average duration, beneficial when rates spiked. High-yield bonds have outperformed VTIP, but at massively increased risk.

Overall, the fact that VTIP's expected returns are higher is more important, if more uncertain, than the fund's past track-record, but the track-record does matter somewhat.

VTIP versus JAAA - Quick Comparison

VTIP compares favorably to most treasuries, the fund's closest peers. VTIP looks slightly worse than the Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA), one of my top income ETFs, however.

JAAA invests in AAA-rated CLOs, securities of broadly similar credit quality to treasuries. JAAA's rate risk or exposure is lower, as CLOs are variable rate investments. JAAA is not protected against inflation in any way, but its lower duration ends up having a similar effect (because rates tend to go up when inflation increases). JAAA's dividend yield is strongly dependent on Fed rates but should settle in the 4.0% - 5.0% range in the coming years, assuming Fed guidance is broadly accurate. Under current conditions, I don't expect significant outperformance from either ETF.

Considering the above, it seems VTIP benefits more strongly from rising inflation and declining rates than JAAA, and vice versa. Although this is something of an odd combination, the Fed tends to increase rates when inflation rises, it occurred during 2021:

During which VTIP outperformed, as expected:

Looking at their overall track-records, JAAA has outperformed VTIP since inception, and with significantly lower volatility to boot. JAAA's outperformance was mostly due to its lower duration, although the fund has had a higher yield than VTIP during most of its existence.

In my opinion, JAAA is a stronger investment than VTIP, as their expected returns are similar, but JAAA's risk and volatility are both lower. JAAA does not seem to be significantly stronger than VTIP, and the latter has some other advantages too, so I still rate VTIP a buy.

Conclusion

VTIP's good expected returns, performance track-record, and inflation protection, make the fund a buy.
